title: The effects of government intervention in different market structures
description: Practice what you've learned about options available to regulate markets where the socially optimal quantity is not produced.
topic_path: Market failure and the role of government
The effects of government intervention in different market structures
The effects of government intervention in different market structures

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following policy interventions will theoretically reduce the deadweight loss generated by a monopoly?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a per-unit subsidy to the monopolist
a lump-sum subsidy to the monopolist
a per-unit tax on the monopolist
a lump-sum tax on the monopolist
a binding price floor on the monopolist

assessment_items.question_content:
A per unit tax is placed on a monopoly with a downward sloping demand curve.

**What happens to the price consumers pay and to deadweight loss?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Price increases; deadweight loss increases
Price increases; deadweight loss doesn’t change
Price decreases; deadweight loss increases
Price doesn’t change; deadweight loss increases
Price decreases; deadweight loss decreases

assessment_items.question_content:
Mega-Conglomerate is a monopoly producer of bread in Hamsterville. Citizens are angry about the inefficiency that this firm generates, and have demanded that the government impose a per-unit tax on Mega-Conglomerate to punish the firm. 

**What happens to the quantity produced and deadweight loss as a result of the tax?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Quantity decreases; deadweight loss increases
Quantity decreases; deadweight loss decreases
Quantity increases; deadweight loss increases
Quantity increases; deadweight loss decreases
Quantity doesn’t change; deadweight loss decreases

assessment_items.question_content:
A government has intervened in the monopoly market shown in this graph. The intervention has caused a change in the firm’s marginal cost curve from $MC_1$ to $MC_2$.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/01b96ecb1ac42af98aa9423509e65a4f.png)

**Which of the following interventions could cause this change, and what is the effect on deadweight loss?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A per-unit tax; deadweight loss increases
A per-unit subsidy; deadweight loss decreases
A lump-sum tax; deadweight loss increases
A lump-sum subsidy; no change in deadweight loss
A binding price ceiling; no change in deadweight loss

assessment_items.question_content:
A per-unit subsidy is given to a monopolist, which causes the shift in marginal cost from MC_1 to MC_2 shown in this graph:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/08b824520b7a46d55e96caeae6040bd81949125a)

**What happens to quantity produced and deadweight loss as a result of the subsidy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Quantity increases from $Q_y$ to $Q_x$; deadweight loss is eliminated
Quantity decreases from $Q_x$ to $Q_y$; deadweight loss is eliminated
Quantity decreases from $Q_x$ to $Q_y$; deadweight loss increases
Quantity increases from $Q_x$ to $Q_y$; deadweight loss is unchanged
Quantity increases from $Q_y$ to $Q_x$; deadweight loss increases

assessment_items.question_content:
A government gives a lump-sum subsidy to a monopolist. 

**What is the effect on profit, the quantity produced, and deadweight loss?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Profit increases; quantity doesn’t change; deadweight loss doesn’t change
Profit increases; quantity increases; deadweight loss decreases
Profit decreases; quantity doesn’t change; deadweight loss decreases
Profit doesn’t change; quantity decreases; deadweight loss increases
Profit decreases; quantity doesn’t change; deadweight loss doesn’t change

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the average total cost (ATC), marginal cost (MC), demand (D), and marginal revenue (MR) for a natural monopoly.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fc9001cb5b7fc8c5670da3fb86b33b0f6becf214)

**If the government regulates the price in this market to P_3, which of the following statements is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The firm earns no economic profit and deadweight loss is reduced, but not eliminated.
The firm earns positive economic profit and deadweight loss is eliminated.
The firm produces the allocatively efficient quantity but will require a subsidy to remain in the industry.
The firm produces a cost-minimizing quantity.
The firm will exit in the industry in the long run.

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the average total cost (ATC), marginal cost (MC), demand (D), and marginal revenue (MR) for a natural monopoly.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fc9001cb5b7fc8c5670da3fb86b33b0f6becf214)

**What is necessary for this firm to be willing to produce the allocatively efficient quantity?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a price ceiling of $P_6$ and a lump-sum subsidy of $P_4deP_6$
No intervention is necessary.
a price floor of $P_1$ and a lump-sum tax of $P_1abP_5$
a regulated price of P_3
There is no allocatively efficient quantity for this firm.

assessment_items.question_content:
**A binding price floor increases inefficiency in all of the following situations EXCEPT**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a monopsony labor market
a perfectly competitive capital market
a monopoly
a perfectly competitive goods market
a monopolistically competitive firm

assessment_items.question_content:
The government of Hamsterville implements a series of laws as a form of antitrust policy. 

**What is the government most likely trying to do?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: limit the ability of firms to create or protect monopoly power
reduce the double counting of output in calculating national income
reduce income inequality between groups
limit the provision of public goods
increase economies of scale
